subject: LU-398 ptlrpc: Add the NRS CRR-N policy

values.-1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this
values.+1: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

messages.message:
Patch Set 1:

I had to drop the CRR-E policy for now, as it was hitting some assertions; I am not sure why, it is almost as if obd_eport structs are being switched during an RPC's lifetime. I will look into it again, although the functionality between CRR-E and CRR-N is the same when clients have mounted the filesystem only once.

The assertions are the same as referenced in my 01/Mar/12 11:19 AM comment in the NRS Jira ticket; we can debug that separately to this code.

Patch Set 1:

Build Started http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11566/

Patch Set 1: Fails

Build Failed 
 
http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11566/ : FAILURE
Uploaded patch set 2.

Patch Set 2:

This patch is the same, it relies on the NRS framework patch being landed first in order to build successfully. The build for this patch will fail, so if someone could disable it, that would be great.

Patch Set 2: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

Nikitas, same deal here.  This patch should be on the same branch as the patch(es) that it is dependent upon.  That way it can be built and tested before the dependent patches are landed.
Uploaded patch set 3.
Uploaded patch set 4.

Patch Set 4:

Build Started http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11911/

Patch Set 4: Verified

Build Successful 
 
http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11911/ : SUCCESS

Patch Set 4:

Similar to the ORR and TRR policies, this CRR-N policy may need a method to recycle struct nrs_crrn_client instances so that they do not end up occupying large amounts of memory. I don't think this should be as serious an issue here as it is for the ORR policy, as I suspect the set of connected clients (as identified by their NIDs, i.e. CRR-N stands for Client Round Robin over NIDs) to a server, should stay relatively stable even over long periods of uptime. All comments and suggestions are welcome of course.

Patch Set 4: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

Uploaded patch set 6.

Patch Set 6:

I have added batched request scheduling behaviour to this policy, as per Andreas' comment; the default batch size is 8, but this is configurable via lprocfs. I have not verified that this policy now behaves as it should with respect to requets scheduling (i.e. that it indeed does batch-schedule RPCs) as I do not have immediate access to a testing cluster, but I am quite sure that it does, since it uses exactly the same mechanism as the ORR/TRR policies, and I have verified that those do behave as expected.

Build Successful 
 
http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/12009/ : SUCCESS

Patch Set 5:

Test abandoned because later commit 12010 found

Patch Set 6:

For batch sizes, I could imagine that for a client doing readdir() + statahead that it could more efficiently get the stat() results for multiple inodes from disk, on the assumption that they are located in close proximity on disk.

Given that there are usually many threads for each service, and a single client cannot consume all of the threads for the service, it makes sense to complete all of the requests for a single client (e.g. 8 by default) before moving on to the next one.

Uploaded patch set 10.

Patch Set 10:

Could someone please advise on whether nrs_crrn_client instances should be freed in this policy when there are no more active references on them? i.e. when a given client does not have any pending RPCs, should its corresponding structure be freed, as is done for nrs_orr_object instances in the ORR/TRR policies? I think something like this (or maybe an LRU-based method or similar) makes sense, but I'm just trying to see how much overhead could be avoided.

Patch Set 10:

Build Started http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/12381/

Patch Set 10: Verified

Build Successful 
 
http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/12381/ : SUCCESS

Patch Set 10:

Rosso Autotest has started the test process

Patch Set 10: Verified

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.3/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/7ee91830-646b-11e2-bfb2-52540035b04c).  Ran 12 tests.  No failures.

Patch Set 10:

Nikitas, per your comment about freeing the CRR instance information - I don't have a strong opinion either way.

I think it makes sense to free it when the refcount is dropped, since this would more closely match e.g. the ORR policy and probably others in the future, and would avoid pinning more memory than needed if there are many clients.  I don't think having a single alloc/free for the client per "burst" of IO is a problem at all (we do this on a per _request_ basis already).

Freeing the CRR instance when the refcount is dropped is _definitely_ preferable to the case where the CRR instance is kept around after the client is disconnected, or if the LRU adds overhead or lock contention on a single lock due to the need to keep the list in order.

Patch Set 10:

Thanks Andreas, I will refresh the patch with this change by tomorrow.

Patch Set 10: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

parents.subject: LU-3057 llite: swap layout fixes - hang at sanity 56x and 56w

commit.subject: LU-398 ptlrpc: Add the NRS CRR-N policy
commit.message:
LU-398 ptlrpc: Add the NRS CRR-N policy

The CRR-N (Client-based Round Robin over NIDs) policy batch-schedules
all types of RPCs in a Round Robin manner according to the NID of the
client that generated the RPC; the maximum size of the batches is
configurable via interaction with lprocfs. The policy aims to provide
for better resource utilization across the cluster, and to help
shorten completion times of jobs in some cases by distributing
available bandwidth more evenly across client nodes.

Signed-off-by: Nikitas Angelinas <nikitas_angelinas@xyratex.com>
Co-authored-by: Liang Zhen <liang@whamcloud.com>
Change-Id: Ie91ee277fc95564908b20fd0d539a274089657ed
Oracle-bug-id: b=13634
Xyratex-bug-id: MRP-73
